item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most of my problems with this scip have been stated above (and probably more eloquently too!) so I'll leave it at this is barely anomalous and the near impossibility of the Foundation linking some of these victims doesn't help the situation. -1

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Reversion Notice
item.content:encoded:
Do remember that there is no standard for amnestic classification, nor for any other similar fictional classification schemes.

Oh, my apologies for that edit - I just forgot that "there is no canon" in SCP articles.

item.title: Staff Post: Reversion Notice
item.content:encoded: Version 11 of this article has been reverted, as it made content edits without authorial permission. Do remember that there is no standard for amnestic classification, nor for any other similar fictional classification schemes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know if this is a "good" article, but it certainly is fascinating, because it has let to a lot of complex and educated analysis.
My thoughts… 1211 itself isn't a psychological condition at all. It is an infrequent anomalous event that creates tenuously connected quartets simultaneously affected by an unique pathology with some similarities to BIID. The rewrite staff pointed out all the reasons this isn't BIID, most importantly that it overrides the body's self identification to grant self-ness and other-ness to internal organs. The ways this doesn't work within normally understood neurology give it a subtle (and perhaps too subtle) anomalous character.
As noted, in BIID, people rarely self-amputate. This means that the condition induced by 1311 is not only sudden and strong, but carries frightening urgency. One might also suppose some subconscious compulsion to conduct the removal away from witnesses and to leave the offending limb or organ intact.
A few people wondered why the Foundation would track homicides. I assumed that deaths characterized by the removal of organs or limbs from the victim and the crime scene would generally be assumed to be murder by investigating law enforcement.

I agree that the SCP probably needs a rewrite, because whether or not I understood things correctly, it should be plainly laid out in the article. This SCP would require personnel across the Foundation to keep one eye open for occurrences, and that would require a clear and concise summary report.

No vote for now, but I think it has still has potential with a rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gender

The state of being male or female (typically used with reference to social and cultural differences rather than biological ones).

Common mistake and not really a problem, but I find it a problem (Obviously)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This kind of reminds me of SCP-1993, in all honestly. Not extremely similar, just some similar elements

item.title: **Rewrite Staff Review**
item.content:encoded:
The author called for a review by the Rewrite Team. Here's my assessment.

Your assessment has been assessed and found lacking in spoiler tags
ftfy- Scantron

All stationed personnel currently assigned to SCP-1311 are to monitor all local homicide police reports for evidence of SCP-1311 outbreaks. Instances of SCP-1311’s pattern being positively identified should be reported immediately. Any witnesses of positively identified SCP-1311 outbreaks are to be given Class-C Amnestics and have their accounts to local authorities modified as necessary. In extreme cases, Class-A Amnestics and relocation are permissible.

If this is supposed to be body integrity identity disorder but "spooky" why are we looking into homicides? Shouldn't these wounds be self-inflicted?

SCP-1311 initially manifests as a case of sudden, severe body integrity disorder—the belief that part of an individual’s body is not actually theirs—in people with no previously recorded history of such a condition.

"Body Identity Integrity Disorder" or BIID is the proper name for this disorder. Also many people go without documented cases because they are embarrassed, afraid, poor or in under served regions. The claim that these people have sudden BIID doesn't really gel with how the disease is reported or works. Some people will go around pretending to be amputees which adds another layer to the pathology of the disorder.

This is also not BIID as currently understood. Those symptoms are the ones you experience when you have Depersonalization Disorder or Derealization disorder.

In most instances, SCP-1311 affects the perception of one’s hands or feet, though in some cases, fingers, ears, eyes, teeth or even organs have also been subject to the condition.

Actually in most instances BIID affects the left leg. It's strange that organs are affected too because organs aren't part of the "mapping" that the brain does. You don't really have proprioception of organs. You have a different set of receptors that monitor internal functions but they don't really "map" to your sense of self. That's why you don't instinctively know where all your organs are or what they do.

SCP-1311 was previously regarded as mere coincidence, but the repeated instances of SCP-1311’s more anomalous aspects led Foundation operatives to require additional research, which in turn caused its final classification.

I wonder if this is actually just coincidence.

As with most cases of acute body integrity disorder, the majority of subjects infected with SCP-1311 have attempted to self-amputate or remove the affected part of the body themselves, resulting in their death. In the cases where a minor part of the body is targeted and successfully amputated, all suffers have expressed a profound feeling of relief at its removal. No survivors of major removals have currently been recovered.

Uh… Normally people don't try to self amputate. They try to goad surgeons into removing the limb by damaging it. Also if these are self-inflicted why would homicide detectives get involved? Do homicide detectives cover suicides (I don't actually know this. It's a real question.)

Further research was first suggested when Dr. ██████ noted that, in all cases where an infected individual was successful in removing the afflicted part of their body, that part of the body was never located. Survivors of the condition universally claimed to have “not noticed” or to “have forgotten” what happened to the body part after removal, though they continue to express happiness that it is gone. No removals have yet been observed or recorded by Foundation personnel.

This is the first sign we have that something interesting is happening beyond the freakish stuff but it doesn't go any further.

Most often, outbreaks of SCP-1311 are discovered after the fact by tracking murder cases involving limb amputation or dismemberment, usually classified as such due to the missing body parts. To date, at least forty-three (43) outbreaks have been recorded around the world, with only the outbreak in █████████ happening at the same location more than once.

Sure… but are these actually murders? Are the four people getting into amputation pacts or something?

other outbreaks have possessed links as tenuous as:

* Eating the same meal for lunch (at different locations);
* Being treated for nail biting;
* Having been a member of the same online community, [REDACTED] (said community is now being monitored, due to its connection to SCP-1311 correlating to a significant increase in the site's popularity);
* Being allergic to apples;
* Being the child of a pancreatic cancer survivor;
* Having sex with the same woman (the woman showed no signs of the condition herself, though she is currently in custody for observation and further testing);
* Simultaneously reading the same book, █████ ██████ and the ████-█████ ██████.

How would the Foundation discover the content of these people's lunches with any certainty? How would they get a comprehensive list of sexual partners?
How would they find out that these people were reading the same book?

These things seem like spurious correlations.

The full list of currently missing body parts currently includes:

* Sixteen arms (hands intact).
* Twelve legs (feet intact).
* Thirty-six hands.
* Seventy-two teeth, including canines, incisors, and molars in various quantities.
* Four sets of genitals, both genders.1
* Twelve kidneys.
* Four lungs.
* Eight spleens.
* Three livers.2

The inclusion of all body parts aside from the limbs and hands seems insane. The genitalia thing… that looks like pathological levels of gender identity disorder. Also how does one "remove" one's labia+vagina? It's a complex organ. Do they continue and try to carve out the uterus and ovaries too?

TLDR: The mechanics of this make no sense and they are based on a really shaky understanding of BIID. They also appear to ignore the way that the body understands itself. The lack of a non-amputated/dead subject is also a problem because it denies us, the readers, from understanding the psychology of this disorder. They can't all be dead or 'forgetful'. Hell, why did the Foundation not interview people with amputated limbs about their experiences before the disorder, during the disorder and after the amputation? Seems like an oversight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like this one. Not very anomalous, no hook, and SCP-1993 did this effect far better.
Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are tone issues and a cross link to 1993 in this. -1.
edit: I meant to say that I didn't find the concept very interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most of what I'd say has already been said by previous commenters. I do want to add that, despite the mysterious circumstances of onset, this is a real mental illness that causes real suffering in many people; it makes me uncomfortable to have it turned into an SCP. And I don't mean "uncomfortable in the good way."

Of course, this is just my opinion and your mileage etc. etc., but I still downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah. Underlining goes back to the typewriter, which couldn't, of course, use italics. Nowadays we don't have that problem anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Book titles have traditionally been underlined, but with the more recent advent of word processing, they've moved toward using italics, largely because it's sexy as hell and easy to do now days. While the early 90's had it listed as optional, using underlining or italics, the MLA reboot in 2009 pretty well…

I just realized that no one gives a damn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. I always thought that book titles were underlined and short story titles were in quotes. Live and learn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The book's title should be in italics (which I don't think works with black boxes, but hey, lets try it), and I bow to the second point. Editing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Simultaneously reading the same book, █████ ██████ and the ████-█████ ██████.

Shouldn't the book's title be in quotes?

To date, ██ outbreaks have been recorded around the world

I think this should be unexpunged. We now have the full list of missing body parts, and so can easily calculate the number of outbreaks.

I think I like the current version enough to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dig it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not for extra classes, myself, but I do appreciate articles that are written in a "We know most of what this does, but we still need more testing" sort of way, which you can totally do with something that's classified. Just a thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Additional classes would be interesting, or novel at the very least. But I don't know that they fit with the quasi-governmental/military structure of The Foundation. Beuracratic heirarchies love their pigeon holes; and it is my experience that they will just keep on hammering until every square peg fits into one of those round holes. Common sense or aptness of description is not a factor, it's all about being able to check a box on a report.

More on topic: I like what you've done here. It's right on the edge of being non-anomalous, but there are enough quirks that I think it fits. I approve of not being afraid to cross-link, but I don't know that 1993 is fitting. Its effect is all about legs, why would they investigate a connection when 1311 victims are cutting off all sorts of other bits? I would have liked just a little bit more about the fate of the severed parts. Not neccessarily a full explanation, but maybe some unconfirmed reports or traces that tentatively point to some possible theories.

Clive Barker has a short story about sentient hands getting themselves severed and staging an uprising against the humans who oppress/enslave them. The rebellion fails, because hands are apparantly kind of stupid, but it inspires other body parts to rise up and free themelves as well. I've decided to believe that is what is happening here. Various body parts are seceding from the union and wandering off to find their own fortunes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sir, I don't know what you're talking about. Ms. Rowling's texts are not up for discussion! And they don't cause self-dismemberment!

The having sex with the same woman bit has to do with the fact that.. four people had sex with the same woman. That's it. I didn't specify gender or situation. Note that it always happens in groups of four.

Unclassified was testing the waters. I'm interested in seeing new kinds of Object Classes, personally, and this seemed like a decent was to try and sow a few seeds for people to push what is, really, the most structured part of the writing. Why only get four or five choices? I want more. I personally see the Foundation as frozen in time. I know what happens in the future of this article; I just think it's more interesting right now.

And I accept your final criticism.

Thanks for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince, eh?

Having sex with the same woman

Because they all like women?

Why unclassified? Why write an article in the midst of something being studied? (Not that I object to that in general, but it doesn't seem to fit here.) Why the link to 1993? It feels tacked on. I'm gonna go with "poor execution" on this one. Making a fairly anomalous real condition a little extra anomalous isn't working in this case. It's not enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since policy is changing on cross-referencing SCPs…

SCP-897

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the alteration to that first sentence of the description changes everything. it no longer "is" the mental condition, but just "manifests" as it. that's much better. upvoted because i liked everything before but felt iffy because of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wat

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In instances were recovery

I refuse to upvote an article with a typo such as this.

Edit: Upvoted now that that is fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am not a real doctor and I agree with what Sorts just said

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: -EX stands for EXplained. This is not explained, because self-mutilated body parts generally don't disappear on their own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I might have a tale for that if this one survives… Hmm…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was actually waiting for something akin to that. Like the body parts would all be different and there would be SOMETHING about a patchwork being made of different body parts.
Kind of like Dr. Frankenstein using a machine that makes people go slightly crazy in the described way and he uses the body parts for his monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm Dr Sorts and I agree with what ChazzK just said

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. I like it BECAUSE there's no twist or Scary Door angle. And yes it's a real condition, but it's a real condition that's spontaneously generated in exactly four people simultaneously whose body parts then vanish. It's not the CONDITION that's anomalous, it's the cause and aftereffects that are.

Getting your neck broken isn't anomalous. Getting your neck broken by a concrete statue that can only move when you're not looking at it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Point 1: It's the sudden impulse to chop off/cut out a (likely not-insignificant) portion of your body, from people with absolutely no prior or subsequent signs of OCD or any other related mental illness. It always pops up in four simultaneous people at the same time, who were doing the same thing (which I'll address below) and cut off the same part (I think.) That's beyond coincidence, and mental disorders of that caliber do not typically just pop into existence for a single, isolated incident, and then vanish completely forever.

They're able to determine that everyone was doing the same thing at the same time, because four different people in four different parts of the country/world all were asked "So what exactly prompted you to chop off your left thumb?" and their answers were were *all* some permutation of "Well, I was just sitting there eating a BLT, when I got this sensation that my thumb wasn't really MY thumb, and it had to go." Again, that's well beyond coincidence territory, especially when it happens again and again. Well I guess they can't exactly ask that if the people bleed to death, or if they feel like their larynx or lower mandible is the part that needs to be excised, but you get the point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "anomalous" bits feel tacked-on, and this article underscores one of the more subtle dangers of crosslinking- even if you have a good reason (as this SCP seems to) for doing it, there's always the risk that the article you're linking is better. In this case, I feel like SCP-1993 is a much better take on the concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yes! thats where i was hoping where we were headed. that an SCP would make!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This would work better as an -EX.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Needs a list of parts. Bonus WTF points if the parts would build one person….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a real mental illness with a few details that could be speciously be called anomalous. There's really not enough here to warrant SCP classification, in my opinion. It's just too easy to explain away as a weird coincidence or an extranormal event at most. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't an SCP; this is an unfortunate mental disorder with some tenous links added. What happened to correlation not being causation? -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't forget that it always occurs in fours, and at the same time.

And yes, it is a reference to the wiki. Mann gets credit for the 'posting in the same online community' part, I get credit for implying that it's related to our boom in popularity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To me it feels like someone writing up obsessive-compulsive disorder and treating it like a memetic disease

THANK you. I couldn't figure out what was bothering me about this, and you just put your finger on it. There really is a psychological disorder where people start thinking that parts of their body aren't theirs, and yes, some have gone to the extreme of having those body parts amputated or removed. It's… disturbing, but explainable.

The only really anomalous parts of this are that the removed body parts disappear and the "woo… they're connected in ways that are incredibly hard to verify!" I mean, really. How would you determine that everyone who was affected were all eating the same meal or reading the same book when they were affected.

Edit: Although I do want to believe that the "being a member of the same online community which experienced a boom in popularity" is referring to this wiki itself. Dang it, the SCP Foundation itself is a cognitohazard/infohazard trigger!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand what he was trying to do, but it seems like this article relies too much on yours to support it rather than being something interesting on its own.

I just don't like the idea. If this was instead a longer tale expanding on the idea, I would be more interested, but I just don't like it as an article. I'm sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In all cases where an infectee is successful in removing the afflicted part of their body, that part of the body is never located.

You…you know this doesn't usually happen, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know how I feels about this one, mainly because this is a documented condition that affects real people. I'm thinking that the Foundation is doing a rather poor job of containment if they are allowing putting these people on TV. To me it feels like someone writing up obsessive-compulsive disorder and treating it like a memetic disease.

There's no twist, there's little to no interpretation, and for me it feels the same as someone writing up their favorite superhero/video game character into an SCP. Downvote.

Edit: Oh, and it links to SCP-1993 for no reason other than they both involve compulsive amputation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a fan of simple SCPs like this, with no tricks or twists, just simple weird crap. But….I feel like something's missing from this. I don't know what it is, but I wasn't left feeling much. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wholly don't understand it. Possibly because it's 7 am and I've had no sleep. It just doesn't get me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The crosslinking works well here, and the. Ompulsions are logical. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. Changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 's good, but I think the paragraph describing links should be in bullet format, because I like lists and they look nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one is great. The reason I like it is the implication that since it's not fully understood, and can only be reacted to, there's room for Keter potential in there. Six degrees of separation plus self-harm? The mind boggles.
